guide.title: Use policy detail to manage the rule and its approved deviations together
guide.summary: The policy record is the governance anchor. Exceptions are subordinate records used when a deviation is deliberately accepted.
body: Maintain the policy statement, linked control, review cadence, owners, and documents in the policy workspace.
Create an exception only when the organization is deliberately allowing a bounded deviation.
Use exception state, expiry, rationale, and compensating control notes to keep the deviation reviewable.

concepts.label: Policy
concepts.summary: A governed statement of expected practice, ownership, and review cadence.
concepts.why_it_exists: Policies provide the rule baseline that other records, such as controls and exceptions, can refer back to.
how_to_use: Keep the statement, version, owners, linked control, and review due date current.
Use attached documents for the working material or approved policy artifacts.

relations.summary: Policies can point to the control they govern or justify.
One policy can have multiple approved or requested exceptions over time.

concepts.label: Policy Exception
concepts.summary: A formally recorded decision to allow deviation from an expected policy or rule.
concepts.why_it_exists: Organizations sometimes accept limited deviations. A policy exception keeps that decision visible, reviewed, and time-bound.
how_to_use: Use an exception when a rule cannot be followed and the deviation is being accepted deliberately.
Record the reason, owner, approval state, expiry, and compensating controls.

relations.summary: Exceptions are managed in the context of one policy.
An exception can reference a finding that triggered or justified it.
An exception may leave risk partially untreated.
